32	# Hiive Deal Pipeline Monitoring Agent Constitution
33	
34	## Core Principles
35	
36	### I. Depth Over Breadth, via MVP-First Sequencing (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
37	
38	One deeply built thing beats three shallow things. The MVP slice defined in `BUILD_PLAN.md`
39	Section 9.1 MUST be built and polished before any stretch work begins. When choosing the next
40	task, the answer is always "the MVP slice item that unblocks the most downstream MVP work" —
41	never "the exciting stretch feature." Stretch items carry the hard-start cut-off hours in
42	`BUILD_PLAN.md` Section 9.2; if an item has not been started by its cut-off, it is abandoned
43	for this submission. **No stretch item may be partial-shipped.** A half-built Screen 3 or a
44	half-built fourth intervention is strictly worse than none.
45	
46	**Rationale:** A 60-hour time box rewards a coherent, polished slice over a broad, broken one.
47	The cut-off discipline protects the MVP from stretch creep in the final hours.
48	
49	### II. Human-in-the-Loop Is Non-Negotiable (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
50	
51	The agent drafts, flags, suggests, prioritizes, and escalates. The agent MUST NOT:
52	- send external communications (emails, client messages, issuer notices),
53	- mutate deal status, stage, or any field in a system of record,
54	- take any irreversible action,
55	
56	without explicit human approval captured in the analyst interface. Every intervention the
57	agent produces is a draft awaiting review. Approval is an affirmative, logged human action —
58	never a default, never a timeout auto-confirm.
59	
60	**Rationale:** Hiive is a FINRA-member, SEC-registered broker-dealer. This is a product-level
61	and regulatory constraint that does not bend, even in a prototype.
62	
63	### III. Domain-Accurate Language
64	
65	Every prompt, every drafted email, every state name, every schema field, every log line,
66	every UI label MUST use Hiive's actual vocabulary: *listing, bid, issuer, ROFR, transfer
67	agreement, accreditation, settlement, breakage, stage, aging, Transaction Services, secondary,
68	pre-IPO*. Generic business-speak ("the deal is progressing," "please advance the opportunity")
69	is a **defect**, not a style choice. When in doubt, re-read the domain primer in
70	`PROJECT_CONTEXT.md` and prefer the narrower Hiive term.
71	
72	**Rationale:** Reviewers are domain experts. Language accuracy is the fastest signal of
73	whether the builder understood the business.
74	
75	### IV. Reasoning Is Explainable and Inspectable
76	
77	Every risk flag, every drafted intervention, every priority ranking, every severity
78	assignment MUST carry the reasoning that produced it, attached to the output in a form the
79	analyst — and a compliance reviewer — can audit. This means structured rationale fields on
80	every agent output, citations to the underlying evidence (which email, which aging threshold,
81	which ROFR clock), and a visible chain from input signal to output recommendation. **Black-box
82	outputs are not acceptable.**
83	
84	**Rationale:** Explainability is both a product requirement (analysts must trust and verify)
85	and a regulated-industry expectation (compliance must reconstruct why an action was taken).
86	
87	### V. Reliability Patterns Are First-Class
88	
89	Even as a prototype, the system MUST demonstrate production-shape engineering:
90	- **Schema-validated outputs** (Pydantic or equivalent) on every LLM call,
91	- **Bounded LLM output spaces** — enums, constrained fields, length caps — not free-form prose
92	  where structure is required,
93	- **Timeouts and retries** with explicit policies on every external call (LLM, data store),
94	- **Idempotency** on the monitoring loop so restarts do not duplicate interventions or drafts,
95	- **Structured logs** with correlation IDs tying agent runs to deals and interventions.
96	
97	The Delivery Voice Agent reliability bar on the builder's resume is the reference standard.
98	
99	**Rationale:** A prototype that looks production-shaped reads as engineering maturity. A
100	prototype that crashes on restart reads as a demo. We are building the former.
101	
102	### VI. Evaluation Is Part of the Product
103	
104	A seeded golden set of deal scenarios with known-correct agent behavior ships **alongside**
105	the agent, not after. The Tier 1 deterministic harness (15 scenarios for MVP per
106	`BUILD_PLAN.md` Section 7.1) runs via `make eval` and produces a scorecard. "Did the agent
107	catch the stall we designed into the data?" MUST be a testable question from the first
108	engineered-issue deal onward. Evaluation code, fixtures, and expected outputs live in the
109	repo and are reviewer-runnable. The ICBC LLM evaluation pipeline on the builder's resume is
110	the reference.
111	
112	**Rationale:** An evaluation harness is the proof that the agent does what it claims. Without
113	it, every behavioral claim is a promise; with it, every claim is a test.
114	
115	### VII. Reviewer-Readable Code
116	
117	A senior engineer at Hiive opens this repo and follows it in ten minutes. That requires:
118	a real README with setup/run/eval/demo instructions; sensible top-level structure; small,
119	focused modules; types where they clarify intent; comments only where they earn their place
120	(non-obvious invariants, ROFR timing edge cases, regulatory constraints). **No code-golf, no
121	speculative abstractions, no cleverness without purpose, no half-finished scaffolding.**
122	When in doubt, prefer the boring, legible option.
123	
124	**Rationale:** The submission is evaluated by humans reading code under time pressure. Every
125	minute spent deciphering cleverness is a minute not spent being impressed.
126	
127	### VIII. Honest About Assumptions
128	
129	Any time the build assumes something about how Hiive actually operates — because internal
130	access is not available — the assumption MUST be documented at the point it is made: in a
131	code comment, in the README's "Assumptions" section, and (for load-bearing ones) in the
132	400-word writeup. Examples: ROFR clock mechanics, Transaction Services email cadence,
133	internal escalation channels, how "stalled" is defined operationally. **Unflagged assumptions
134	are defects.**
135	
136	**Rationale:** A good consultant is explicit about the seams between what they know and what
137	they have inferred. This signals judgment and invites correction rather than hiding gaps.
138	
139	### IX. Synthetic Data Must Be Convincing
140	
141	The demo stands or falls on whether the simulated pipeline feels real. Therefore:
142	- Issuers MUST be real Hiive-listed companies or plausible analogs, not placeholder names.
143	- Generated emails MUST read like Transaction Services correspondence — appropriate register,
144	  realistic phrasing, domain-accurate detail — not generic business copy.
145	- Deadlines, ROFR windows, and aging thresholds MUST reflect realistic mechanics, not
146	  arbitrary durations.
147	- Variety is **engineered** (distribution of stages, deal sizes, issues, aging), not random.
148	
149	The five engineered-issue scenarios in `BUILD_PLAN.md` Section 6.3 are the canonical bar.
150	
151	**Rationale:** A reviewer who sees a toy data set stops taking the system seriously. A
152	reviewer who sees a plausible one evaluates the agent on its merits.
153	
154	### X. Submission-Shaped From Day One
155	
156	The assignment asks for: a runnable output, a 400-word writeup, a one-paragraph reflection,
157	and optional coding session transcripts. Every build decision MUST answer the question:
158	**"does this help or hurt the submission?"** The goal is not the best possible system in
159	abstract — it is the best possible submission that demonstrates engineering depth in the
160	available time. Polish the README, the `make demo` path, and the writeup alongside the code,
161	not after.
162	
163	**Rationale:** Engineering invisible to the reviewer does not count. Submission shape is a
164	first-class product concern.
165	
166	## Project Constraints
167	
168	**Regulated-industry prototype.** Hiive is a FINRA-member, SEC-registered broker-dealer.
169	Principle II (HITL) and Principle IV (explainability) are compliance constraints, not product
170	preferences. The agent's scope is strictly advisory: observe, reason, draft, flag, escalate.
171	
172	**Time box.** 60 hours total. MVP slice targets hour 55 (per `BUILD_PLAN.md` Section 10.1).
173	Stretch queue operates under the cut-offs in Section 9.2. Scope valves in Section 9.4 are
174	contingency, not plan.
175	
176	**Reference materials.** `PROJECT_CONTEXT.md` (domain primer, problem framing) and
177	`BUILD_PLAN.md` (scope, sequencing, MVP vs. stretch) are the authoritative scope documents.
178	When this constitution and those documents disagree, this constitution governs on *principles*
179	and those documents govern on *scope and sequencing*.
180	
181	## Development Workflow
182	
183	**MVP-first sequencing.** No stretch work is started until the MVP slice in `BUILD_PLAN.md`
184	Section 9.1 is complete and polished. The decision protocol in Section 9.3 (hour-40 and
185	per-cut-off checks) is followed literally.
186	
187	**Evaluation alongside implementation.** Each new risk dimension, severity level, or
188	intervention type ships with at least one golden-set scenario that exercises it (Principle VI).
189	PRs/commits that add behavior without a corresponding scenario are incomplete.
190	
191	**Assumption log.** A running list of assumptions lives in `README.md` (or equivalent) and is
192	updated as assumptions are made (Principle VIII). The 400-word writeup surfaces the
193	load-bearing ones.
194	
195	**Domain-language review.** Before merging any prompt, email template, state name, or UI
196	label, check against Principle III. Generic wording is rewritten in Hiive vocabulary before
197	shipping.
198	
199	**Submission checks.** At hours 40, 50, and 55, verify: `make setup`, `make seed`, `make run`,
200	`make eval`, `make demo`, and `make clean` all work from a clean clone; README is current;
201	writeup and reflection drafts exist.
202	
203	## Governance
204	
205	This constitution supersedes other practices and preferences for this project. All
206	`/speckit.specify`, `/speckit.plan`, `/speckit.tasks`, and `/speckit.implement` work MUST
207	operate under these principles.
208	
209	**Conflict resolution.** When a downstream decision (spec, plan, task, implementation) would
210	conflict with a principle, the conflict MUST be flagged explicitly in the artifact and
211	resolved — by adjusting the decision, by narrowing scope, or (rarely) by amending the
212	constitution — **before** proceeding. Silent compromise is prohibited.
213	
214	**Amendments.** Amendments require: (1) a written rationale in the amending commit, (2) a
215	version bump per the policy below, and (3) a Sync Impact Report updating affected templates
216	and docs. Principle removals or redefinitions require a MAJOR bump and an explicit note in
217	the writeup if the submission relies on the amended principle.
218	
219	**Versioning policy (semantic).**
220	- **MAJOR**: Backward-incompatible principle removal or redefinition.
221	- **MINOR**: New principle added, or a principle materially expanded.
222	- **PATCH**: Clarifications, wording fixes, non-semantic refinements.
223	
224	**Compliance review.** Every agent-produced artifact surfaced to a human reviewer MUST carry
225	its rationale (Principle IV). Every external action MUST be gated on explicit human approval
226	(Principle II). Any PR that weakens these gates is rejected.
227	
228	**Runtime guidance.** Day-to-day build guidance lives in `BUILD_PLAN.md` and `PROJECT_CONTEXT.md`.
229	This constitution governs *how* decisions are made; those documents govern *what* is built.
230	
231	**Version**: 1.0.0 | **Ratified**: 2026-04-16 | **Last Amended**: 2026-04-16
232
